one_line_takeaway: SAGE-LLM pairs a fuzzy control-barrier-function safety filter (which certifies discretized directional cones around LLM-issued semantic actions) with a star-hierarchical graph RAG for domain knowledge, achieving near-zero collision rates across multiple LLM backbones in a simulated UAV pursuit-evasion task -- this is a control-safety/verification paper, not an AI-security (adversarial threat/attack) paper, so it is off-topic for an AI-security knowledge graph despite surface use of the word 'safety'.
key_finding: In the safety-verification experiment (Table 1), SAGE-LLM reached 90% success / 99.73% safe rate / 92% zero-danger-epoch, versus DDPG (92%/90.57%/52%), Negative-Reward (56%/92.93%/60%), Primal-Dual (60%/97.52%/48%), and MSMAR-RL (64%/98.29%/80%) -- i.e. SAGE-LLM had the best safety and zero-danger rate while giving up only 2 points of success rate versus the unconstrained DDPG baseline. In the architecture-robustness experiment (Table 2), safe rate stayed above ~98.5% across all six backbones/scales (Qwen-max 80%/99.68%/85%, Qwen3-235b 70%/99.11%/70%, Qwen3-32b 75%/99.13%/70%, Qwen3-8b 50%/98.55%/65%, DeepSeek Chat 75%/99.51%/80%, GLM-4.5 75%/99.71%/85%), showing safety is decoupled from backbone reasoning capacity while task success scales with model size. The ablation (Table 3) showed: full system 80%/99.68%/85.00%; Fuzzy-CBF-only 55%/99.05%/90.00%; Graph-RAG-only 70%/97.12%/45.00%; neither module 50%/98.81%/60.00% -- indicating Fuzzy-CBF alone is safety-heavy but conservative (lower success), Graph-RAG alone raises success but encourages riskier maneuvers (lower zero-danger rate), and the two modules are complementary.
core_contribution: SAGE-LLM is a train-free, two-layer LLM controller architecture for UAV pursuit-evasion decision-making that combines (1) a Fuzzy Control Barrier Function (Fuzzy-CBF) verification mechanism that models each discrete LLM action as a directional uncertainty cone (a semantic action defined as a triple (v_k, theta_k, V_k) over a spherical-cap admissible direction set), discretizes the cone into basis directions, and constructs a mixed CBF via convex combination to give a formal safety certificate for LLM-generated high-level instructions; and (2) a Star-Hierarchical Graph (SHG) RAG system that organizes UAV decision knowledge into a scene hub node plus hierarchical policy/control subgraphs (N = N_S ∪ N_H ∪ N_L) with a hybrid neural-symbolic node-scoring function Phi(n,q_l)=alpha*cos(z_v,z_ql)+(1-alpha)*I_syn, retrieved via top-down layer-by-layer search that maximizes a log-likelihood path score. At runtime, an LLM produces a high-level plan; if the Fuzzy-CBF classifies it unsafe, an LLM-driven low-level safety controller executes an avoidance maneuver instead of the RL pursuit policy.
limitations: The paper itself only states forward-looking limitations/future work: it aims to optimize computational efficiency for real-time deployment on resource-constrained embedded platforms, and plans to extend to multi-UAV swarm coordination and other embodied-AI domains (ground robotics, autonomous driving). It does not report inference latency, does not test against dynamic/moving obstacles (only a moving target drone amid static obstacles), and the safety math is stated to converge via convex combination with the full proof deferred to an appendix not included in the extracted pages.

datasets_used: No external dataset; evaluation is entirely in a self-built NVIDIA Isaac Gym UAV pursuit-evasion simulation (RTX 4090 GPUs) with configurable spherical/cylindrical obstacle layouts
quantitative_results: Table 1 (Safety Verification, 5 fixed random spheres): SAGE-LLM 90% success / 99.73% safe rate / 92% zero-danger-epoch
Table 1 baselines: DDPG 92%/90.57%/52%; Negative-Reward 56%/92.93%/60%; Primal-Dual 60%/97.52%/48%; MSMAR-RL 64%/98.29%/80%

Table 2 (Architecture Robustness, obstacle-position randomization): Qwen-max 80%/99.68%/85%; Qwen3-235b 70%/99.11%/70%; Qwen3-32b 75%/99.13%/70%; Qwen3-8b 50%/98.55%/65%; DeepSeek Chat 75%/99.51%/80%; GLM-4.5 75%/99.71%/85%
Table 3 (Ablation): Fuzzy-CBF+Graph-RAG 80%/99.68%/85.00%; Fuzzy-CBF only 55%/99.05%/90.00%; Graph-RAG only 70%/97.12%/45.00%; neither 50%/98.81%/60.00%
baselines_compared: Negative Reward / collision-penalty Safe RL (Qu et al., 2023)
MSMAR-RL, a SOTA safe-RL recovery-strategy method (Zhao et al., 2025) that is also authored by overlapping authors of this paper
